title: NATO Integrates Drones in Latest Major Exercises in the Baltic, Mediterranean Seas
description: NATO is working to integrate emerging technologies like uncrewed surface vehicles and fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicles into crisis planning through its latest major exercises in the Baltic and Mediterranean seas. text:
NATO is working to integrate emerging technologies like uncrewed surface vehicles and fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicles into crisis planning through its latest major exercises in the Baltic and Mediterranean seas.
Dynamic Manta, NATO’s premier anti-submarine warfare exercise, launched Monday in the Mediterranean Sea, bringing together 10 allied nations. The exercise, which runs through March 6, will include the use of an uncrewed surface vehicle (USV) as NATO works to integrate emerging technologies.
Overall, the exercise will see participation from submarines, maritime patrol aircraft, helicopters and surface ships from Canada, France, Germany, Greece, Italy, Portugal, Spain, Turkey, the U.K. and the U.S., according to a NATO news release. The exercise is designed to enhance interoperability and operational effectiveness across the full spectrum of maritime operations.
“Dynamic Manta provides an unmatched opportunity for allied submarines, surface forces and air assets to train together as one team. This exercise enhances our collective anti-submarine warfare capabilities and ensures NATO remains ready to defend allied waters today and in the future,” U.S. Navy Rear Adm. Bret Grabbe, Commander, Submarines NATO, said in a NATO news release.
Standing NATO Maritime Group Two (SNMG2) will play a central role in Exercise Dynamic Manta, providing maritime command and control and enabling close integration and coordination of multinational surface forces, submarines and air assets. SNMG2 currently includes Italian Navy frigate ITS Virginio Fasan (F591) as the flagship, along with Hellenic Navy frigate HS Limnos (F451) and German Navy fleet oiler FGS Elbe (A511).
Beyond SNMG2’s participation, no details have been officially released on the ships, submarines and aircraft participating in the drill.
Turkish state media Anadolu Agency reported Turkey is deploying submarine TCG B.İnönü (S360) and a P-72 MPA for the Mediterranean exercise. A NATO Maritime Command post on social media channel X on Dynamic Manta 26 showed an unidentified Turkish submarine, Italian frigate Virginio Fasan, Italian Navy fleet oiler ITS Vulcano (A5335) and Spanish Navy frigate ESPS Navarra (F85).
Dynamic Mantra comes on the heels of Exercise Steadfast Dart 26, a NATO exercise in the Baltic Sea and Germany. Emerging technology was also on display during that exercise as Turkey, for the first time, deployed a fixed-wing UAV from the flight deck of an amphibious assault ship with the Bayraktar TB-3 UAV. The UAV was also used Feb. 20 to conduct counter-Unmanned Aircraft System (c-UAS) training for NATO aircraft over the Baltic Sea, according to a NATO release. The drills involved German and Italian Eurofighter and Spanish F-18 fighter aircraft, supported by a Spanish A400M tanker.
Overall, Steadfast Dart 26 was aimed at testing NATO’s ability to rapidly deploy and employ its Allied Reaction Force (ARF) in a crisis scenario across land, sea, air, space and cyber domains. The exercise saw the participation of 10,000 troops and more than 15 ships from 13 NATO countries.
Last week on Feb. 18, an amphibious landing in the Putlos military training area in northern Germany was conducted as part of Steadfast Dart 26. The amphibious force was led by Spanish Navy amphibious landing platform dock ESPS Castilla (L52) with embarked Spanish Special Operations Forces.
The rest of the amphibious force consisted of the Turkish Maritime Task Group – including amphibious assault ship TCG Anadolu (L-400) with embarked Turkish Marines – frigates TCG Istanbul (F-515) and TCG Oruçreis (F-245), fast combat support ship TCG Derya (A-1590), Spanish Navy frigate ESPS Cristóbal Colón (F105) and SNMG1.
For the exercise, SNMG1 was composed of Spanish Navy frigate ESPS Almirante Juan de Borbon (F102), French Navy frigate FS Commandant Blaison (F793), German Navy frigate FGS Sachsen (F219), Spanish Navy fleet oiler ESPS Patiño (A14) and Standing NATO Mine Countermeasures Group One (SNMCMG1). The countermeasures group included Polish Navy mine countermeasures command ship ORP Kontradmiral Xawery Czernicki (511), German Navy minehunter FGS Fulda (M1058) and Royal Netherlands Navy minehunter HNLMS Schiedam (M860).
During the exercise, the ships of SNMCMG1 conducted mine countermeasures drills, securing and clearing the area of operations, ensuring safe access and approach routes for the amphibious ships.
According to a NATO release, the amphibious landing operation began with the launch of a Bayraktar TB-3 unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) from Anadolu, providing real-time intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance over the objective area. Simulated hostile targets identified by the UAV were subsequently engaged by German Eurofighter 2000 aircraft.
Spanish Special Operations Forces then conducted an underwater insertion to clear simulated explosive hazards along the shoreline. Turkish and Spanish naval special operations teams fast-roped ashore by helicopter, securing key positions and preparing the objective for the main landing force, while Turkish attack helicopters provided close air support.
The main landing force followed in with Turkish Marines approaching the beach in high-speed boats, followed by ZAHA amphibious assault vehicles. Landing craft conducted an additional landing east of the main effort, reinforcing the beachhead and expanding the operational footprint. The scenario concluded with the extraction of Spanish Special Operations Forces using an Airborne Tactical Extraction Platform, reads the release.
With the conclusion of Steadfast Dart 26, most naval units involved are now participating in the German Navy’s Northern Quadriga 2026 exercise in the North Sea. The exercise is focused on protecting merchant shipping against maritime threats.
NATO and its member countries have been moving toward hosting multinational exercises in consecutive succession in order to maximize the use of deployed forces.
Following Quadriga 2026, the Turkish Maritime Task Group along with ships from other NATO nations will head to Norway to take part in NATO exercise Cold Response 2026. That exercise is expected to see 25,000 military personnel from 14 NATO countries take part. The field phase of the drills will run March 9-19. U.S. Marines and U.K. Royal Marine Commandos are already in Norway conducting drills in preparation for Cold Response 2026.
